questions.question: What is the best response to '¿Cuántos años tienes?'
questions.explanation: '¿Cuántos años tienes?' asks your age, so 'Tengo veinte años' (I'm twenty) answers it.
questions.theory: Spanish expresses age with 'tener' (to have): 'Tengo... años' literally means 'I have... years.'

questions.question: Which word means 'Monday' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: 'Lunes' is Monday.
questions.theory: Days of the week in Spanish are not capitalized: lunes (Monday), martes (Tuesday), etc.

questions.question: Which word means 'today' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: 'Hoy' means 'today'.
questions.theory: Time words include ayer (yesterday), hoy (today), and mañana (tomorrow).

questions.question: What does 'mañana' mean when it refers to a day?
questions.explanation: As a day word, 'mañana' means 'tomorrow' (it can also mean 'morning').
questions.theory: 'Mañana' has two meanings: 'tomorrow' (the day) and 'morning' (part of the day), known from context.

questions.question: Which word means the month 'January' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: 'Enero' is January.
questions.theory: Spanish months are lowercase: enero (January), febrero (February), marzo (March).

questions.question: In a restaurant, how do you politely say 'I would like a coffee'?
questions.explanation: 'Quiero un café, por favor' (I want a coffee, please) is a natural, polite order.
questions.theory: When ordering, 'quiero' (I want) plus 'por favor' is common; 'quisiera' is even more polite.

questions.question: What does a waiter mean by '¿Qué desea?'
questions.explanation: '¿Qué desea?' means 'What would you like?' (literally 'What do you wish?').
questions.theory: Servers often ask '¿Qué desea?' or '¿Qué desean?' to take your order politely.

questions.question: How do you ask for the bill (check) in a restaurant?
questions.explanation: 'La cuenta, por favor' means 'The bill, please.'
questions.theory: 'La cuenta' is the restaurant bill. Adding 'por favor' makes the request polite.

questions.question: What does 'el menú' mean in a restaurant?
questions.explanation: 'El menú' is 'the menu'.
questions.theory: Useful restaurant words: el menú (menu), la cuenta (bill), el camarero/mesero (waiter).

questions.question: What does someone wish you when they say 'Buen provecho' at a meal?
questions.explanation: 'Buen provecho' means 'enjoy your meal' (similar to 'bon appétit').
questions.theory: 'Buen provecho' is said before or during a meal to wish others an enjoyable meal.

questions.question: How do you say 'a glass of water' when ordering?
questions.explanation: 'Un vaso de agua' means 'a glass of water.'
questions.theory: 'Vaso' is a drinking glass and 'agua' is water, so 'un vaso de agua' orders a glass of water.

questions.question: How do you ask 'Where is the bathroom?' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: '¿Dónde está el baño?' means 'Where is the bathroom?'
questions.theory: To ask where something is, use '¿Dónde está...?' plus the place, such as 'el baño' (the bathroom).

questions.question: If someone says 'Siga todo recto,' what should you do?
questions.explanation: 'Siga todo recto' means 'go straight ahead.'
questions.theory: Common direction phrases: 'todo recto' (straight ahead), 'a la derecha' (right), 'a la izquierda' (left).

questions.question: What does 'a la derecha' mean?
questions.explanation: 'A la derecha' means 'to the right.'
questions.theory: 'Derecha' means right and 'izquierda' means left, key words for following directions.

questions.question: What does 'a la izquierda' mean?
questions.explanation: 'A la izquierda' means 'to the left.'
questions.theory: When asking for directions, 'a la izquierda' tells you to go or turn to the left.

questions.question: How do you ask 'How much does it cost?' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: '¿Cuánto cuesta?' means 'How much does it cost?'
questions.theory: Use '¿Cuánto cuesta?' to ask the price of one item; '¿Cuánto cuestan?' for plural items.

questions.question: What is the best response to '¿Hablas inglés?'
questions.explanation: '¿Hablas inglés?' asks if you speak English, so 'Sí, hablo inglés' (Yes, I speak English) answers it.
questions.theory: 'Hablar' means 'to speak.' '¿Hablas inglés?' asks 'Do you speak English?' answered with sí or no.

questions.question: 'Sí' means 'yes' and 'no' means 'no' in Spanish.
questions.explanation: 'Sí' (with an accent) means 'yes' and 'no' means 'no.'
questions.theory: The accent on 'sí' (yes) distinguishes it from 'si' (if). 'No' looks the same in both languages.

questions.question: How do you say 'I don't understand' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: 'No entiendo' means 'I don't understand.'
questions.theory: 'Entender' means 'to understand,' so 'no entiendo' is a useful phrase when you are confused.

questions.question: How do you politely ask someone to repeat: 'Can you repeat, please?'
questions.explanation: '¿Puede repetir, por favor?' means 'Can you repeat, please?'
questions.theory: 'Puede' (you can, formal) plus an infinitive makes polite requests, like '¿Puede repetir?'

questions.question: What is the best response to '¿Qué día es hoy?' if today is Tuesday?
questions.explanation: '¿Qué día es hoy?' asks what day it is, so 'Hoy es martes' (Today is Tuesday) answers it.
questions.theory: '¿Qué día es hoy?' means 'What day is today?' You answer with 'Hoy es...' plus the day.

questions.question: How do you say 'Welcome!' (to one person) in Spanish?
questions.explanation: '¡Bienvenido!' means 'Welcome!' when addressing a man or in general.
questions.theory: 'Bienvenido' welcomes someone; it changes form to bienvenida/bienvenidos/bienvenidas to match the person(s).

questions.question: 'Por favor' means 'please' in Spanish.
questions.explanation: 'Por favor' is the standard phrase for 'please.'
questions.theory: Adding 'por favor' to a request makes it polite, just as 'please' does in English.

questions.question: You should reply 'Son las una' to say 'It is one o'clock.'
questions.explanation: One o'clock is singular: the correct form is 'Es la una,' not 'Son las una.'
questions.theory: Only one o'clock uses the singular 'es la una'; every other hour uses the plural 'son las...'.

questions.question: How do you say 'See you later' in Spanish?
questions.explanation: 'Hasta luego' means 'see you later.'
questions.theory: 'Hasta luego' is a friendly farewell used when you expect to see the person again soon.

questions.question: How do you ask someone's name politely using the formal 'you'?
questions.explanation: '¿Cómo se llama usted?' is the formal way to ask 'What is your name?'
questions.theory: Spanish has informal (tú) and formal (usted) forms; with usted you say '¿Cómo se llama?'

questions.question: What does '¿Cómo se dice... en español?' ask?
options: How do you say... in Spanish?
questions.explanation: '¿Cómo se dice... en español?' means 'How do you say... in Spanish?'
questions.theory: This phrase is handy for learning new words: '¿Cómo se dice 'book' en español?'

questions.question: What is a natural reply to 'Buenas tardes' from a shopkeeper?
questions.explanation: The simplest, most natural reply to 'Buenas tardes' is to greet back with 'Buenas tardes.'
questions.theory: Spanish greetings are usually echoed back: when greeted with 'buenas tardes,' you reply the same way.

questions.question: How do you say 'I'm sorry, I don't speak Spanish well'?
questions.explanation: 'Perdón, no hablo bien español' means 'Sorry, I don't speak Spanish well.'
questions.theory: 'Perdón' apologizes, and 'no hablo bien español' explains a limited level, useful for travelers.

questions.question: At a restaurant the waiter asks: "¿Qué le pongo?" What is he asking?
options: What can I get you (to order)?
questions.explanation: "¿Qué le pongo?" is a common way for servers to ask what you would like to order.
questions.theory: The verb "poner" is used colloquially in service contexts to mean serving or bringing food and drinks. "¿Qué le pongo?" literally asks 'what shall I put (down) for you?'

questions.question: Someone says "Muchas gracias por todo." The most natural reply is:
questions.explanation: "No hay de qué" means 'don't mention it / you're welcome,' a natural response to thanks.
questions.theory: Spanish has several ways to say 'you're welcome': "de nada," "no hay de qué," and "con gusto." They acknowledge gratitude and downplay the favor.

questions.question: You want to pay and call the waiter: "¿Me trae la cuenta, por favor?" What did you ask for?
questions.explanation: "La cuenta" is the bill/check at a restaurant.
questions.theory: "Traer" (to bring) is common in polite restaurant requests. "¿Me trae...?" softens a request by using the present tense as a courtesy.

questions.question: In a shop the clerk asks: "¿Algo más?" You have everything you need. You answer:
questions.explanation: "Eso es todo" means 'that's all,' the right reply when you need nothing else.
questions.theory: "¿Algo más?" ('Anything else?') is a routine shopping question. The standard closing reply is "eso es todo" or "nada más."

questions.question: A friend says "Te invito a un café." What does this most likely mean?
options: I'm inviting you to my house.
I want you to make coffee.
I'll treat you to a coffee (my treat).
questions.explanation: "Invitar" in this context means to treat/pay for someone, not merely to invite.
questions.theory: In Spanish "te invito" usually implies the speaker will pay. It is a warm, common way to offer to buy someone a drink or meal.

questions.question: Choosing the polite way to ask a stranger for directions:
questions.explanation: "Disculpe, ¿cómo llego...?" uses a polite opener and a complete, courteous question.
questions.theory: "Disculpe" (formal 'excuse me') is the standard way to approach a stranger. Pairing it with "¿cómo llego a...?" makes a clear, polite request for directions.

questions.question: At a hotel desk you say: "Tengo una reserva a nombre de López." What are you doing?
questions.explanation: Stating a reservation 'a nombre de' (under the name of) is how you check in.
questions.theory: "A nombre de" means 'under the name of.' It is the phrase used when claiming a booking at hotels, restaurants, or ticket counters.

questions.question: Someone sneezes and you say "¡Salud!" What does it express?
questions.explanation: "¡Salud!" said after a sneeze is the equivalent of 'bless you.'
questions.theory: "Salud" literally means 'health.' It works as both a toast ('cheers') and the response to a sneeze, similar to 'bless you.'

questions.question: You step in front of someone to pass by. The appropriate phrase is:
questions.explanation: "Con permiso" is said when passing by or excusing yourself to move through.
questions.theory: "Con permiso" asks permission to pass or leave. It differs from "perdón/disculpe," which is used to get attention or apologize for a bump.

questions.question: At the table someone says "¡Buen provecho!" This means:
questions.explanation: "Buen provecho" is the Spanish equivalent of 'enjoy your meal / bon appétit.'
questions.theory: "Buen provecho" is said before or during eating to wish someone an enjoyable meal. It is polite to say it when joining or leaving people who are dining.

questions.question: On the phone you hear "¿De parte de quién?" The caller is being asked:
questions.explanation: "¿De parte de quién?" asks who is calling / on whose behalf.
questions.theory: This is standard telephone Spanish. "De parte de" means 'on behalf of,' so the speaker wants to know the caller's identity before connecting them.

questions.question: You try on shoes and say "Me quedan un poco grandes." What's the problem?
options: They are a bit too big for you.
questions.explanation: "Me quedan grandes" means the shoes fit (on you) too big.
questions.theory: "Quedar" with clothing describes fit. "Me queda(n) grande/pequeño/bien" expresses whether an item is too big, too small, or fits well.

questions.question: At a café: "—¿Para tomar aquí o para llevar? —Para llevar." What did the customer choose?
questions.explanation: "Para llevar" means 'to go / takeaway.'
questions.theory: "Para tomar aquí" means 'to have here' and "para llevar" means 'to take away.' These phrases are essential at cafés and fast-food counters.

questions.question: True or False: "¿Me cobra, por favor?" is a natural way to ask to pay the bill.
questions.explanation: "¿Me cobra?" asks the server to charge you, i.e., to take your payment.
questions.theory: "Cobrar" means 'to charge (money).' Saying "¿Me cobra?" is a common, polite way to signal you're ready to pay, especially in Spain.

questions.question: Your friend looks sad. The most caring thing to say is:
questions.explanation: "¿Qué te pasa?" means 'What's wrong / what's the matter with you?'
questions.theory: "Pasar" with an indirect object asks what is happening to someone. "¿Qué te pasa?" shows concern about someone's mood or situation.

questions.question: At the pharmacy: "Me duele la garganta y tengo tos." What's wrong?
questions.explanation: "Garganta" is throat and "tos" is cough, so it's a sore throat with a cough.
questions.theory: "Me duele + (body part)" expresses pain. Common pharmacy vocabulary includes "garganta" (throat), "cabeza" (head), and "tos" (cough).

questions.question: Someone asks "¿Te importa si abro la ventana?" You agree. Best reply:
questions.explanation: "No, adelante" means 'No (I don't mind), go ahead.'
questions.theory: "¿Te importa si...?" ('Do you mind if...?') is answered with "no" to give permission, since you're saying it doesn't bother you. "Adelante" means 'go ahead.'

questions.question: At the train station: "¿De qué andén sale el tren a Sevilla?" You want to know the:
questions.explanation: "Andén" means platform (track) at a station.
questions.theory: "Andén" is the platform where trains arrive and depart. "¿De qué andén sale...?" asks which platform a train leaves from.

questions.question: You didn't understand and want someone to repeat. The most polite option is:
questions.explanation: "¿Cómo dice? ¿Puede repetir, por favor?" politely asks for repetition.
questions.theory: "¿Cómo dice?" (formal) or "¿Cómo?" politely signals you didn't catch something. Adding "¿puede repetir, por favor?" makes the request courteous.

questions.question: A clerk says "Lo siento, está agotado." What does that mean about the product?
questions.explanation: "Agotado" means sold out / out of stock.
questions.theory: "Agotado" describes something that has run out or sold out. It is common in shops when an item is unavailable.

questions.question: True or False: If a host says "Estás en tu casa," they are telling you to leave.
questions.explanation: "Estás en tu casa" means 'make yourself at home,' a warm welcome, not a dismissal.
questions.theory: "Mi casa es tu casa" / "estás en tu casa" is a hospitable expression inviting a guest to feel comfortable. It signals welcome, not departure.

questions.question: At the market you ask "¿A cómo está el kilo de tomates?" You want to know:
questions.explanation: "¿A cómo está...?" asks the current price of something sold by weight.
questions.theory: "¿A cómo está(n)...?" is a common Latin American way to ask the going price, especially for produce sold per kilo. In Spain "¿a cuánto está?" is also heard.

questions.question: You bump into someone by accident. The best immediate reaction is:
questions.explanation: "¡Perdón! ¿Está bien?" apologizes and checks if the person is okay.
questions.theory: "Perdón" is used to apologize for a bump or mistake. Following it with "¿está bien?" politely checks on the other person.

questions.question: A waiter asks "¿Cómo quiere el filete?" and you want it well done. You say:
questions.explanation: "Bien hecho" means well done when describing how meat is cooked.
questions.theory: Cooking levels for steak include "poco hecho" (rare), "al punto" (medium), and "bien hecho" (well done). "¿Cómo quiere el filete?" asks your preference.

questions.question: On leaving a shop the clerk says "Que tenga un buen día." A fitting reply is:
questions.explanation: "Igualmente" ('you too / likewise') returns the good wish.
questions.theory: "Igualmente" mirrors a good wish back to the other person. It's the natural response to phrases like "que tenga un buen día."

questions.question: You ask for the wifi: "¿Me podría dar la contraseña del wifi?" What did you request?
questions.explanation: "La contraseña del wifi" is the wifi password.
questions.theory: "Contraseña" means password. "¿Me podría dar...?" uses the conditional to make a very polite request.

questions.question: True or False: "¿Me da uno de esos?" while pointing at pastries is a natural way to order one.
questions.explanation: "¿Me da uno de esos?" ('Can you give me one of those?') is natural when pointing.
questions.theory: "¿Me da...?" (literally 'will you give me...?') is a common, polite request form in shops and bakeries. Pointing with "uno de esos" handles unknown item names.

questions.question: A friend invites you out and you must decline. The polite refusal is:
options: Me encantaría, pero no puedo hoy.
questions.explanation: "Me encantaría, pero no puedo hoy" softens a refusal with regret.
questions.theory: Polite refusals often start with appreciation ('I'd love to') before stating you can't. This preserves the relationship while declining.

questions.question: At customs: "¿Algo que declarar?" The traveler with nothing to declare says:
questions.explanation: "No, nada" means 'No, nothing (to declare).'
questions.theory: "¿Algo que declarar?" is standard customs language. A traveler with nothing taxable replies "nada que declarar" or simply "no, nada."

questions.question: Someone says "Estoy de acuerdo contigo." They are expressing that they:
questions.explanation: "Estar de acuerdo" means to agree.
questions.theory: "Estar de acuerdo (con)" is the fixed expression for agreeing with someone. It contrasts with "no estoy de acuerdo" for disagreement.

questions.question: In a taxi you say "Al aeropuerto, por favor. ¿Cuánto sería?" You are asking:
options: Roughly how much the fare will be
questions.explanation: "¿Cuánto sería?" politely asks the approximate fare.
questions.theory: The conditional "sería" softens the question about price into 'how much would it be?' It's a polite way to ask a taxi fare in advance.

questions.question: A shopkeeper says "Pase por caja." What should you do?
options: Go to the checkout/register to pay
questions.explanation: "La caja" is the cash register/checkout, so you should go pay there.
questions.theory: "Caja" means the cashier or checkout. "Pase por caja" directs a customer to the register to complete the purchase.

questions.question: You want to know if a dish has nuts due to an allergy. Best question:
questions.explanation: "¿Lleva frutos secos?" asks if the dish contains nuts.
questions.theory: "Llevar" is used for ingredients a dish 'carries/contains.' "Frutos secos" covers nuts, key vocabulary for allergy questions.

questions.question: Your friend passes an exam. The right thing to say is:
questions.explanation: "¡Felicidades!" means 'Congratulations!'
questions.theory: "Felicidades" (or "enhorabuena" in Spain) congratulates someone on an achievement. "Lo siento" is for sympathy and "socorro" is a cry for help.

questions.question: At the bank: "Quería cambiar dólares a euros. ¿A cuánto está el cambio?" You ask about the:
questions.explanation: "El cambio" here refers to the exchange rate.
questions.theory: "Cambio" can mean change, exchange, or rate depending on context. "¿A cuánto está el cambio?" asks the current currency exchange rate.

questions.question: Someone offers you more food: "¿Quieres más?" You're full. Polite reply:
questions.explanation: "Estoy lleno" means 'I'm full,' the natural reason to decline more food.
questions.theory: "Estar lleno/llena" expresses being full after eating. Declining food politely usually pairs "no, gracias" with a brief reason.

questions.question: A colleague says "Te llamo luego, ¿vale?" What does "¿vale?" function as here?
questions.explanation: In Spain "¿vale?" is a tag meaning 'okay?/all right?'
questions.theory: "Vale" is a very common filler/agreement word in Spain, equivalent to 'okay.' As a tag question "¿vale?" seeks confirmation.

questions.question: In a clothing store: "¿Tiene esta camisa en una talla más grande?" You're asking for a:
questions.explanation: "Una talla más grande" means a larger size.
questions.theory: "Talla" is clothing size. Comparative phrases like "una talla más grande/pequeña" ask for a size up or down.

questions.question: You're lost and someone says "Está aquí al lado, a dos minutos andando." The place is:
questions.explanation: "Aquí al lado... andando" means it's right nearby, a short walk away.
questions.theory: "Al lado" means 'next to / right by here,' and "andando" means 'on foot.' Together they signal something is within easy walking distance.

questions.question: True or False: Replying "Que descanses" to someone going to bed means 'rest well.'
questions.explanation: "Que descanses" is a friendly 'rest well / sleep well.'
questions.theory: "Que + present subjunctive" forms good-wish expressions, like "que descanses" (rest well) or "que te mejores" (get well). They convey kind wishes.

questions.question: At a restaurant: "¿Me podría traer otra servilleta?" You need another:
questions.explanation: "Servilleta" means napkin.
questions.theory: Common table items include "servilleta" (napkin), "tenedor" (fork), "cuchillo" (knife), and "cuchara" (spoon). "¿Me podría traer...?" is a polite request.

questions.question: A friend tells you bad news. The most empathetic response is:
questions.explanation: "Cuánto lo siento" expresses deep sympathy.
questions.theory: "Lo siento" expresses regret or sympathy; adding "cuánto" intensifies it. It is the appropriate response to someone's misfortune.

questions.question: Booking a table: "Quería reservar una mesa para cuatro a las nueve." You request a table for:
questions.explanation: "Para cuatro a las nueve" means for four people at nine o'clock.
questions.theory: "Para + number" gives the party size, and "a las + hour" gives the time. The imperfect "quería" softens the request politely.

questions.question: Someone says "¡No me digas!" after your surprising story. They mean:
questions.explanation: "¡No me digas!" is an idiom expressing surprise, like 'you don't say!'
questions.theory: Although literally 'don't tell me,' "¡no me digas!" is a set exclamation of surprise or disbelief, not a real command to stop talking.

questions.question: At the doctor: "¿Desde cuándo se siente así?" The doctor wants to know:
options: Since when you've felt this way
questions.explanation: "¿Desde cuándo...?" asks since when, i.e., how long you've felt that way.
questions.theory: "Desde cuándo" asks about the starting point of a situation. In medical settings it's used to learn how long symptoms have lasted.

questions.question: You want to split the bill with friends. You say:
questions.explanation: "Pagar a medias" means to split the cost.
questions.theory: "A medias" means 'half each / go halves.' "¿Pagamos a medias?" proposes splitting the bill, a common social phrase.

questions.question: A receptionist says "Un momento, le paso con el gerente." What will happen?
options: She'll transfer you to the manager.
questions.explanation: "Le paso con..." means 'I'll put you through to...' on the phone.
questions.theory: "Pasar con" on the telephone means to transfer or connect a caller. "Le paso con el gerente" forwards you to the manager.

questions.question: You want to be addressed informally and say "Podemos tutearnos." This means:
options: We can use 'tú' with each other.
questions.explanation: "Tutearse" means to address each other with the informal 'tú.'
questions.theory: "Tutear" is the verb for using "tú" instead of "usted." Suggesting "podemos tutearnos" proposes switching to informal address.

questions.question: At a shop you ask "¿Aceptan tarjeta o solo efectivo?" You want to know the accepted:
questions.explanation: "Tarjeta" is card and "efectivo" is cash, so you're asking about payment methods.
questions.theory: "Tarjeta" (card) and "efectivo" (cash) are core payment terms. Asking "¿aceptan tarjeta o solo efectivo?" checks how you can pay.

questions.question: A friend leaving on a trip hears you say "¡Buen viaje!" You wished them:
questions.explanation: "¡Buen viaje!" means 'have a good trip.'
questions.theory: "Buen viaje" is the set phrase to wish someone a pleasant journey. Other travel-related wishes include "buen vuelo" (good flight).

questions.question: Your Spanish friend says '¡No me digas!' after you tell her surprising news. What is she most likely expressing?
options: Surprise or disbelief, like 'You don't say!'
A request for more details immediately
questions.explanation: '¡No me digas!' is an idiomatic exclamation of surprise, equivalent to 'No way!' or 'You don't say!', not a literal order.
questions.theory: Many Spanish exclamations are fixed idioms whose meaning differs from their literal words. '¡No me digas!' literally means 'Don't tell me' but conventionally signals astonishment.

questions.question: A waiter in Spain asks '¿Qué le pongo?' Which register and meaning does this convey?
options: Informal; 'What did I put on you?'
questions.explanation: '¿Qué le pongo?' uses the indirect object 'le' (usted) and idiomatically means 'What can I get/serve you?' in a service context.
questions.theory: In service interactions, 'poner' colloquially means 'to serve/get'. The pronoun 'le' marks the formal usted register typical of waiters addressing customers.

questions.question: Which expression best translates the English idiom 'It's not a big deal' in casual Spanish?
options: No es nada grande de comer
questions.explanation: 'No pasa nada' is the natural colloquial way to say 'It's no big deal / Don't worry about it'.
questions.theory: Idioms rarely translate word-for-word. 'No pasa nada' literally means 'nothing happens' but functions as reassurance, equivalent to 'It's fine / no worries'.

questions.question: In Mexico, if someone says '¿Mande?' after you call their name, what do they mean?
options: 'Pardon? / Yes?' — a polite response when addressed
questions.explanation: In Mexico '¿Mande?' is a polite, traditional way to respond to being called, equivalent to 'Yes?' or 'Pardon?'.
questions.theory: Regional usage shapes everyday responses. While literally from 'mandar' (to command), '¿Mande?' in Mexican Spanish is simply a courteous acknowledgment when someone addresses you.

questions.question: True or False: The phrase 'Estoy que me caigo de sueño' idiomatically means the speaker is extremely sleepy.
questions.explanation: 'Caerse de sueño' is an idiom meaning to be so tired you could fall over, i.e., extremely sleepy.
questions.theory: Spanish uses vivid body-based idioms for states. 'Caerse de sueño' (literally 'to fall down from sleepiness') conveys an intense degree of tiredness.

questions.question: Which response is the most natural, native-like reply to '¿Qué tal?' among friends?
options: Estoy muy bien de salud, gracias por preguntar
questions.explanation: 'Nada, aquí ando' (Nothing much, just hanging around) is a casual, idiomatic reply that fits the informal greeting.
questions.theory: Greetings have conventional, often understated responses. Over-formal or literal answers sound unnatural; casual fillers like 'aquí ando' match the register of '¿Qué tal?'.

questions.question: A colleague in Spain ends an email with 'Un saludo'. How should you interpret this?
questions.explanation: 'Un saludo' is a standard, polite, semi-formal email closing roughly equal to 'Regards' or 'Best'.
questions.theory: Written register has its own conventions. 'Un saludo' (and 'Saludos') sits between the formal 'Atentamente' and the warm 'Un abrazo', signaling professional neutrality.

questions.question: In Argentina, the pronoun used to address a friend informally as the subject is:
questions.explanation: Argentine Spanish uses 'vos' (voseo) instead of 'tú' for the informal singular 'you'.
questions.theory: Voseo is a major regional feature in the Río de la Plata area. 'Vos' replaces 'tú' and triggers special verb forms, e.g., 'vos tenés' instead of 'tú tienes'.

questions.question: Someone says 'Me cae bien tu hermano.' What does this convey?
questions.explanation: 'Caer bien' is an idiom meaning to find someone likeable or to get along well with them.
questions.theory: 'Caer bien/mal' expresses interpersonal impressions. Despite 'caer' meaning 'to fall', the idiom describes whether a person makes a good or bad impression on you.

questions.question: Which is the most natural way to politely interrupt and ask someone to repeat themselves in formal Spanish?
questions.explanation: 'Perdone, ¿podría repetírmelo?' uses formal usted and the conditional for politeness, the most appropriate native-like option.
questions.theory: Politeness in Spanish often relies on the conditional ('podría') plus formal pronouns. A bare '¿Qué?' or imperative 'Dilo' would sound abrupt in a formal setting.

questions.question: In Spain, 'Me mola mucho esta canción' means:
options: This song bothers me a lot
questions.explanation: 'Molar' is Spanish (Spain) slang for 'to be cool/to like', so 'me mola' means 'I really like it'.
questions.theory: 'Molar' is colloquial peninsular Spanish equivalent to 'gustar' but cooler in tone. It is widely used among younger speakers in Spain and is largely unknown elsewhere.

questions.question: A friend texts 'Tengo un montón de cosas que hacer, estoy hasta arriba.' What does 'estar hasta arriba' mean here?
options: To be swamped / overwhelmed with work
To be at the top of the rankings
questions.explanation: 'Estar hasta arriba' colloquially means to be overwhelmed or extremely busy, fitting the context of having tons to do.
questions.theory: Idioms with 'hasta' express saturation. 'Estar hasta arriba (de trabajo)' conveys being completely overloaded, similar to the English 'up to my ears'.

questions.question: Choose the most natural way to soften a request: 'Could you possibly help me move this weekend?'
options: Oye, ¿no te importaría echarme una mano con la mudanza este finde?
¿Me ayudas a mudarme este fin de semana, sí o no?
Tienes que ayudarme a mudarme este fin de semana.
questions.explanation: '¿No te importaría echarme una mano...?' uses a negative-conditional softener and the idiom 'echar una mano', sounding polite and natural.
questions.theory: Native speakers soften favors with conditional phrasing and idioms like 'echar una mano' (to lend a hand). Direct imperatives or 'tienes que' come across as demanding.

questions.question: True or False: Saying 'Buenos días' to a single person you address as 'usted' is grammatically required to change to 'Buen día usted'.
questions.explanation: 'Buenos días' is invariable regardless of formality; it does not change form based on tú/usted.
questions.theory: Fixed greetings like 'Buenos días' do not inflect for register. Formality is signaled through pronouns and verb forms elsewhere, not by altering the greeting itself.

questions.question: In a Mexican context, 'Está bien padre tu coche' means:
options: Your father is in your car
Your car belongs to your father
questions.explanation: In Mexican slang 'padre' (or 'padrísimo') means 'cool/great', so the sentence praises the car.
questions.theory: Regional slang repurposes common words. In Mexico 'padre' as an adjective means 'awesome', a usage distinct from its literal meaning of 'father'.

questions.question: Which sentence most naturally expresses 'I'm looking forward to seeing you' to a close friend?
questions.explanation: 'Tener ganas de' is the idiomatic way to express looking forward to or wanting to do something.
questions.theory: English 'look forward to' has no literal Spanish equivalent; calques sound wrong. 'Tener ganas de + infinitive' conveys eager anticipation naturally.

questions.question: Your host says 'Estás en tu casa.' The intended meaning is:
options: You must go back to your own house
You are confused about where you live
This house now belongs to you legally
questions.explanation: 'Estás en tu casa' (or 'mi casa es tu casa') is a warm idiom meaning 'make yourself at home'.
questions.theory: Hospitality formulas are conventionalized. 'Estás en tu casa' is a gracious welcome, not a literal statement about property or location.

questions.question: Which is the most natural, polite way to decline more food at a Spanish dinner table?
options: No, no quiero más, estoy lleno hasta el cuello
No, gracias, estoy muy bien así
questions.explanation: 'No, gracias, estoy muy bien así' is a gracious, native-like refusal that avoids bluntness.
questions.theory: Politely declining relies on softeners and gratitude. Saying you're 'bien así' (fine as is) is more elegant than blunt refusals or graphic statements about fullness.

questions.question: In Spain, 'Vamos a tomar algo' on a Friday night most naturally implies:
options: Let's take something from a store
questions.explanation: 'Tomar algo' colloquially means to go have a drink (or coffee), a standard social invitation.
questions.theory: 'Tomar' (to take) combines with 'algo' to form a vague but understood social formula meaning 'have a drink/something', central to Spanish socializing.

questions.question: A speaker says 'Ese político promete mucho, pero al final, mucho ruido y pocas nueces.' What does the idiom mean?
options: He likes nuts and loud noises
questions.explanation: 'Mucho ruido y pocas nueces' means 'much ado about nothing' — lots of fuss but little real result.
questions.theory: Proverbial idioms compress cultural judgments. 'Mucho ruido y pocas nueces' criticizes empty hype, paralleling Shakespeare's 'much ado about nothing'.

questions.question: When addressing a police officer you don't know in most of Latin America, the most appropriate pronoun choice is:
questions.explanation: With strangers and authority figures, 'usted' is the respectful default in most of the Spanish-speaking world.
questions.theory: Register choice depends on social distance and authority. With unfamiliar officials, 'usted' signals appropriate respect; 'tú/vos' could seem overly familiar or disrespectful.

questions.question: 'Se me hace agua la boca' is best understood as:
options: I spilled water on my face
questions.explanation: 'Hacérsele agua la boca' is the idiom for 'to make one's mouth water' at appetizing food.
questions.theory: This idiom uses a reflexive 'se me' construction to show an involuntary reaction. It precisely mirrors the English 'my mouth waters'.

questions.question: Which sentence best conveys mild annoyance through tone: a friend sighs and says...
options: Tenemos una reunión mañana a las nueve.
Me encantan las reuniones de trabajo.
questions.explanation: '¡Qué bien, otra reunión más!' is sarcastic, using positive words to express irritation about yet another meeting.
questions.theory: Tone and sarcasm reverse literal meaning. The exaggerated 'otra... más' plus a cheery 'qué bien' signals the speaker is actually displeased.

questions.question: In Spain, 'Estoy flipando con lo que me cuentas' means:
options: I'm flipping a coin about it
I'm amazed/blown away by what you're telling me
questions.explanation: 'Flipar' is peninsular slang meaning to be amazed or astonished, so 'estoy flipando' = 'I'm blown away'.
questions.theory: 'Flipar' (from English 'flip') is common Spain slang for strong astonishment. Context determines whether the amazement is positive or shocked.

questions.question: True or False: In Spanish, switching from 'usted' to 'tú' mid-conversation can be invited with the phrase 'Podemos tutearnos'.
questions.explanation: 'Tutearse' means to address each other with 'tú', and 'Podemos tutearnos' is a real invitation to drop formality.
questions.theory: The verb 'tutear' captures the act of using the informal 'tú'. Offering 'podemos tutearnos' is a culturally recognized way to reduce social distance.

questions.question: A friend says 'Me dejaste plantado ayer.' What happened?
options: You stood them up / didn't show
questions.explanation: 'Dejar plantado a alguien' means to stand someone up — failing to meet them as agreed.
questions.theory: 'Dejar plantado' literally evokes leaving someone 'planted' waiting. It is the standard idiom for failing to keep an appointment or date.

questions.question: Which is the most natural way to say 'By the way, did you call the doctor?' in casual conversation?
questions.explanation: 'Por cierto' is the idiomatic discourse marker for 'by the way' introducing a new topic.
questions.theory: Discourse markers organize conversation. 'Por cierto' signals an incidental remark or topic shift, unlike literal 'by the way' calques such as 'por el camino'.

questions.question: In Chile, 'Esa prueba estuvo muy fome.' The word 'fome' means:
questions.explanation: In Chilean Spanish 'fome' means boring or dull, so the test was tedious.
questions.theory: 'Fome' is a quintessential Chilean term for something boring or lame, with no equivalent in most other dialects, illustrating strong regional vocabulary differences.

questions.question: Your friend says 'Lo dijo con toda la cara' about someone lying. The idiom implies the person:
options: Said it shamelessly, with a straight face
questions.explanation: 'Con toda la cara' (or 'tener mucha cara') implies brazenness — saying something shamelessly.
questions.theory: 'Cara' (face) underlies many idioms about nerve or shamelessness. 'Con toda la cara' stresses audacity, similar to 'with a straight face' or 'shamelessly'.

questions.question: Which sentence sounds most natural for 'It's been ages since we last saw each other!'
options: ¡Por mucho tiempo no nos hemos encontrado!
¡Es muchos años desde la última vista!
¡Tiene mucho tiempo que no nos miramos!
questions.explanation: '¡Hace siglos que no nos vemos!' is the natural idiom using 'hace + time' plus the hyperbolic 'siglos' (centuries).
questions.theory: Spanish expresses elapsed time with 'hace + period + que'. The exaggeration 'siglos' adds the emotive 'ages' nuance, a common native-like flourish.

questions.question: A Spaniard says 'Ese tío es un crack.' What is being expressed?
options: That guy is excellent at what he does
questions.explanation: In Spain 'un crack' is praise meaning someone is outstanding or a star at something.
questions.theory: Borrowed English words shift meaning in slang. 'Un crack' in Spain admiringly labels a highly skilled person; 'tío' here just means 'guy', not literally 'uncle'.

questions.question: Which reply best fits the function of accepting an apology graciously?
options: No te preocupes, no pasa nada.
Me da igual lo que digas.
questions.explanation: 'No te preocupes, no pasa nada' warmly accepts the apology and reassures the other person.
questions.theory: Speech acts like accepting apologies have routine formulas. Combining 'no te preocupes' with 'no pasa nada' conveys forgiveness and downplays the offense.

questions.question: 'Tirar la toalla' in a conversation about a tough project means to:
questions.explanation: 'Tirar la toalla' is the idiom 'to throw in the towel', i.e., to give up.
questions.theory: Borrowed from boxing imagery, 'tirar la toalla' means surrendering or quitting. It directly parallels the English 'throw in the towel'.

questions.question: In a formal business email opening to someone you don't know, the most appropriate greeting is:
questions.explanation: 'Estimado/a Sr./Sra.:' is the standard formal salutation for professional correspondence with strangers.
questions.theory: Written formal register has set salutations. 'Estimado/a' signals respect and distance, whereas '¡Hola!' or '¿Qué onda?' are far too casual for first-contact business email.

questions.question: A friend says about a pricey restaurant: 'Nos costó un ojo de la cara.' This means it:
questions.explanation: 'Costar un ojo de la cara' means to cost a fortune — to be very expensive.
questions.theory: Body-part idioms intensify meaning. 'Un ojo de la cara' (an eye from your face) dramatizes high cost, like the English 'cost an arm and a leg'.

questions.question: Which is the most natural way to express '(I) couldn't care less' colloquially in Spain?
questions.explanation: 'Me importa un pimiento' is a colloquial idiom meaning 'I couldn't care less'.
questions.theory: Spanish expresses indifference with idioms naming trivial objects ('un pimiento', 'un bledo'). A literal translation of the English fails; the fixed phrase is required.

questions.question: Reading the dialogue — A: '¿Vienes a la fiesta?' B: 'Ya veremos...' — B's tone most likely signals:
questions.explanation: 'Ya veremos' ('we'll see') is a polite, noncommittal hedge that often implies reluctance.
questions.theory: Vague phrases like 'ya veremos' let speakers avoid a direct 'no'. The tone and indefiniteness usually signal hesitation rather than genuine agreement.

questions.question: In Caribbean and much of Latin American Spanish, '¿Qué lo que?' (often '¿Qué lo que, loco?') functions as:
questions.explanation: '¿Qué lo que?' is Dominican/Caribbean slang for 'What's up?', a casual greeting among friends.
questions.theory: Regional greetings can defy standard grammar. '¿Qué lo que?' is a fixed colloquial salutation, especially Dominican, signaling informality and familiarity.

questions.question: Which sentence most naturally conveys 'Don't beat around the bush' to a friend?
options: No le des vueltas y dime lo que pasa
questions.explanation: 'No le des vueltas' ('stop going around it') is the idiomatic equivalent of 'don't beat around the bush'.
questions.theory: Idioms map concepts, not words. Spanish uses 'dar vueltas' (to go in circles) for evasive talk; literal translations of the English bush idiom are meaningless.

questions.question: A Mexican friend says '¡Aguas!' as you're about to step into a puddle. They mean:
questions.explanation: In Mexican Spanish '¡Aguas!' is a warning meaning 'Watch out! / Be careful!'.
questions.theory: Regional interjections carry idiomatic warnings. '¡Aguas!' historically warned of water thrown from windows and now broadly means 'look out!'.

questions.question: True or False: In the dialogue A: 'Te invito a cenar.' B: 'No, hombre, ¡faltaría más!', B is refusing because they are offended.
questions.explanation: '¡Faltaría más!' here is a warm, polite acceptance/insistence, not an offended refusal.
questions.theory: 'Faltaría más' is a courtesy formula meaning 'of course / it's the least I can do'. Combined with 'no, hombre', it graciously accepts or returns the offer, not rejects it.

questions.question: 'Te lo digo en serio, no es moco de pavo.' The idiom 'no es moco de pavo' means:
questions.explanation: 'No es moco de pavo' means 'it's nothing to sneeze at / it's no small thing'.
questions.theory: This idiom asserts importance. Literally 'it's not turkey snot', it conventionally means a matter is significant and shouldn't be dismissed.

questions.question: Which is the most natural, polite way to ask a stranger for the time in Spain?
questions.explanation: 'Perdona, ¿tienes hora?' is the common, courteous way to ask a stranger for the time.
questions.theory: Politeness routines pair an apology ('perdona/perdone') with an idiomatic question. '¿Tienes hora?' is the conventional phrasing, more natural than a bare '¿Qué hora es?'.

questions.question: A friend recounts a chaotic day and concludes 'En fin, fue un día de locos.' 'En fin' here functions to:
questions.explanation: 'En fin' is a discourse marker used to conclude or sum up, similar to 'anyway' or 'in short'.
questions.theory: Discourse connectors structure narration. 'En fin' signals closure or resignation, drawing a story to a close, distinct from its literal 'at the end'.

questions.question: True or False: The expression 'ponerse las pilas' tells someone to relax and take it easy.
questions.explanation: 'Ponerse las pilas' means to get one's act together or step it up, the opposite of relaxing.
questions.theory: 'Ponerse las pilas' (literally 'put in your batteries') urges energy and effort. It is an exhortation to be more active and focused, not to slow down.

questions.question: A friend says 'Estoy hasta las narices de esperar.' The tone conveyed is:
questions.explanation: 'Estar hasta las narices' means to be fed up, expressing clear exasperation.
questions.theory: Saturation idioms with body parts ('hasta las narices', 'hasta la coronilla') express being fed up. They strongly mark irritation and impatience in tone.

questions.question: Which sentence most naturally renders 'I'll take a rain check' (politely postpone) into Spanish?
options: Lo dejamos para otro día, ¿te parece?
questions.explanation: 'Lo dejamos para otro día, ¿te parece?' naturally postpones the plan, the functional equivalent of a rain check.
questions.theory: Functional translation captures intent over words. The English 'rain check' has no Spanish calque; 'dejarlo para otro día' politely reschedules.

questions.question: In the exchange A: 'Perdón por llegar tarde.' B: 'Tranqui, no te rayes.', B's reply (in Spain) means:
questions.explanation: 'No te rayes' is Spain slang for 'don't stress / don't overthink it', and 'tranqui' is short for 'tranquilo'.
questions.theory: Clipped slang ('tranqui') and idioms ('rayarse' = to obsess/worry) mark casual peninsular speech. B is reassuring A not to worry about being late.

questions.question: Which sentence best expresses genuine, warm thanks for a big favor in a native-like way?
options: No sabes cuánto te lo agradezco, de verdad, me has salvado.
Te agradezco el favor número uno.
Muchas gracias por el favor que es grande.
questions.explanation: 'No sabes cuánto te lo agradezco... me has salvado' conveys heartfelt, natural gratitude with idiomatic intensifiers.
questions.theory: Sincere thanks scales with phrasing. Native speakers amplify gratitude with expressions like 'no sabes cuánto' and 'me has salvado', far warmer than a bare 'gracias'.
